patient_history.chief_complaint: rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass on medial right upper extremity in a 41-day-old infant

patient_transcript:
Doctor: Hi, how are you today?
Parent: I'm good, thanks.
Doctor: Great. Can you tell me what brought you here today?
Parent: My baby is here for evaluation of a rapidly enlarging mass on his right arm.
Doctor: I see. Can you tell me a bit more about your baby?
Parent: He's a 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy.
Doctor: And when was the mass first noticed?
Parent: It was about 5 hours ago.
Doctor: And has it been rapidly enlarging since then?
Parent: Yes, it has been getting bigger.
Doctor: Has your baby had any previous medical procedures on that arm?
Parent: Yes, he had a PICC line placed there during the neonatal period for treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus.
Doctor: Understood. What did you notice on the physical examination?
Parent: The mass was pulsatile, but his capillary refill was within 2 seconds, pulses were good in all extremities, his arms and legs were warm, and there was no discoloration or swelling.
Doctor: We obtained a CT angiogram, which showed a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring about 1.8 by 1.6 by 2.1 cm.
Parent: Okay.
Doctor: An MRI then showed a 2.1 by 1.7 by 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow, consistent with a pseudoaneurysm.
Parent: I see.
Doctor: Because of concern for a vascular abnormality related to the prior line, we admitted him to the neonatal intensive care unit for observation. Over the next two days the mass continued to grow, and plastic surgery and vascular surgery were consulted. Your baby is scheduled for microscope-assisted brachial artery repair with both teams involved.
Parent: Thank you for explaining.
chart_notes: 39-week term, 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy presented to the ED with a rapidly enlarging, pulsatile mass on the medial aspect of the right upper extremity. Parents first noticed the mass 5 hours before arrival; it had enlarged rapidly. Neurovascular exam showed a warm, well-perfused limb with 2+ brachial, radial, and ulnar pulses, capillary refill <2 seconds, no discoloration or edema, and age-appropriate movement of all extremities. Past history notable for PICC placement in the same region during the neonatal period for treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus. CT angiography demonstrated a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring approximately 1.8 × 1.6 × 2.1 cm with clear communication to the brachial artery lumen and no other vascular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced MRI confirmed a 2.1 × 1.7 × 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow. The infant was admitted to the NICU for observation; over 2 days the mass continued to grow, and plastic and vascular surgery teams were consulted. Under general anesthesia, the pseudoaneurysm was exposed via curvilinear incision at the elbow, the diseased segment resected, and a tension-free end-to-end microvascular anastomosis of the brachial artery (2 mm diameter) was performed with 9-0 nylon. Postoperatively, the infant received low-dose intravenous heparin (10 U/kg/h) for 48 hours, had warm, well-perfused upper extremities without bleeding, and was discharged on postoperative day 3 with an arm splint and oral acetaminophen suspension for pain. At 2-year follow-up, the surgical site was well healed, brachial/ulnar/radial pulses were symmetric, and right upper extremity function was normal.
